title: IoT Device Provisioning Audit
dataSources: AWS CloudTrail (`sourcetype="aws:cloudtrail"`; filter `eventSource` / `eventName` for IoT control-plane APIs such as `iot.amazonaws.com`), or a custom `iot:provisioning` sourcetype from your pipeline.
app: AWS IoT Fleet / Azure DPS audit
spl:
index=audit sourcetype="iot:provisioning"
| where action IN ("RegisterThing","CreateCertificate","AttachPolicy")
| stats count by actor, device_template, src
| where count > 50
| sort -count
description: Unauthorized provisioning events create shadow devices and billing abuse.
value: Unauthorized provisioning events create shadow devices and billing abuse.
grandmaExplanation: Unauthorized provisioning events create shadow devices and billing abuse ‚Äî so faults, tampering, and safety issues are spotted right away.
implementation: Compare provisioning rate to approved baseline. Alert on new template or IAM principal. Cross-check with HR for contractor access. For AWS, prefer CloudTrail (`sourcetype="aws:cloudtrail"`) with `eventSource="iot.amazonaws.com"` (and relevant `eventName` values) instead of a non-standard `cloudtrail:iot` sourcetype; keep `iot:provisioning` only if that is your normalized pipeline.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: AWS IoT Fleet / Azure DPS audit.
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: AWS CloudTrail (`sourcetype="aws:cloudtrail"`; filter `eventSource` / `eventName` for IoT control-plane APIs such as `iot.amazonaws.com`), or a custom `iot:provisioning` sourcetype from your pipeline..
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Compare provisioning rate to approved baseline. Alert on new template or IAM principal. Cross-check with HR for contractor access. For AWS, prefer CloudTrail (`sourcetype="aws:cloudtrail"`) with `eventSource="iot.amazonaws.com"` (and relevant `eventName` values) instead of a non-standard `cloudtrail:iot` sourcetype; keep `iot:provisioning` only if that is your normalized pipeline.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=audit sourcetype="iot:provisioning"
| where action IN ("RegisterThing","CreateCertificate","AttachPolicy")
| stats count by actor, device_template, src
| where count > 50
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**IoT Device Provisioning Audit** ‚Äî Unauthorized provisioning events create shadow devices and billing abuse.

Documented **Data sources**: AWS CloudTrail (`sourcetype="aws:cloudtrail"`; filter `eventSource` / `eventName` for IoT control-plane APIs such as `iot.amazonaws.com`), or a custom `iot:provisioning` sourcetype from your pipeline. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): AWS IoT Fleet / Azure DPS audit. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: audit; **sourcetype**: iot:provisioning. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=audit, sourcetype="iot:provisioning". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where action IN ("RegisterThing","CreateCertificate","AttachPolicy")` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by actor, device_template, src** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where count > 50` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (provisioning events), Timeline (bursts), Bar chart (by actor).
visualization: Table (provisioning events), Timeline (bursts), Bar chart (by actor).
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern ‚Äî correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
